data.title: Globalstar: Commercial Execution Is Heating Up With New Contracts
data.description:
In my first cover of Globalstar (GSAT) in January 2025, I built a case arguing for Globalstar to be undervalued, and I issued a Strong Buy recommendation. Since that article, Globalstar is up 104%. You can read the article here. I argued that high margin expansion of the Internet of Things, commercialization of XCOM RAN, and the partnership with Apple are the primary catalysts for the stock.

In this article, I will evaluate the milestones Globalstar has achieved since I first wrote about them and how they are performing in their business areas and compare them to my earlier estimates. Due to the significant stock price appreciation, I am adjusting my rating for the medium term from Strong Buy to Buy. However, for the long term, I remain convinced that the stock is a Strong Buy.

XCOM RAN

A core part of my earlier coverage was XCOM RAN. In it, I noted that it enables high-capacity private 5G networks that work on Globalstar's dedicated Band n53 mid-band spectrum. My presumption was that this would play well with the AI-mediated automatization trend that we are currently seeing. I believe GSAT is still perfectly positioned to take advantage of this, and the recent events validate this. For a detailed description of how XCOM RAN works, I suggest reading my previous article.

Since I last wrote about GSAT, there have been 3 developments I’d like to talk about:

Warehouse Automation

In Q3, the company announced the completion of a new supply agreement and receipt of an initial order from a new warehouse automation customer. Although Globalstar didn’t specify who the customer is, the company has previously conducted tests with certain customers, and now we have a signed initial order. I think it is likely that this contract stems from one of those prior collaborations. This validates my theory from the previous article that customers see value in XCOM RAN. I believe that this initial order will result in broader commercial rollout and new contracts. This should provide a bullish catalyst for the stock.

Skydio Partnership

In December 2025, Globalstar and Skydio announced a partnership in the field of autonomous drones. The companies ran a trial that integrated Globalstar’s Band n53 spectrum directly into Skydio’s X10 drones using the XCOM RAN private 5G platform. Note that Skydio is not a niche player but a supplier for the US government.

I believe Skydio's choosing GSAT gives the company another vertical for expansion. Additionally, we have seen that the US military is increasingly relying on drones, and Globalstar is well-positioned to take advantage of this trend.

Boingo Wireless

In February 2026, Globalstar announced a partnership with Boingo Wireless. As stated in the agreement, ''Boingo will integrate XCOM RAN into its private network infrastructure. Boingo’s private networks deliver secure, high-bandwidth connectivity for critical operations at airports, transit hubs, stadiums, hospitals, convention centers, military bases, and more.'' This will, in my opinion, provide Globalstar high-margin revenue, which will likely resemble SaaS-based revenue. In the agreement, it was stated that ''Boingo’s leadership is built on experience—supporting more than 130 major airports and transportation hubs, 70+ stadiums and arenas, and 100 military bases.''

I believe that this will be a huge asset for Globalstar if they manage to execute well. With the Boingo deal, Globalstar’s XCOM RAN is now being used in industrial, public, and military applications. Also, I would like to point out that all these announced contracts were recent; thus, I estimate that 2026 revenues are poised to rise sharply, potentially pushing the price higher.

IoT Segment

In Q3 2025, Globalstar noted that the hardware sales increased 60% YoY. This was due to the release of the RM200M, which enables bi-directional communications. Previous hardware enabled only one-way data transmission; they were used basically as trackers. These new RM200M modules, however, also allow commands to be relayed to the specific hardware they control. For example, a module could be programmed to turn off a generator. We have seen how Palantir’s revenue is accelerating as they enable 2-way communication and automation within networks. The RM200M hardware provides similar command-and-control functions for remote assets

In my opinion, equipment sales are a leading indicator of the future subscription revenues, which I believe will provide the company with high-margin revenues.

Apple Partnership and C3 Constellation

Globalstar plans to launch C3 satellites via SpaceX in the first half of 2026, though we have not yet received specific launch dates. I believe the company will provide an update on the matter in the Q4 earnings call. For those unfamiliar with the matter, these are the upgraded satellites for which Apple has already agreed to reimburse 95% of the CapEx. Feel free to read the previous article where I went into greater detail regarding the partnership.

Valuation

I firmly believe Globalstar will beat analysts projected growth of 12% due to the soaring hardware sales numbers and recently announced XCOM RAN deals. In my opinion it is more reasonable to assume sales growth of 15-20%. Considering this, 2026 revenues would be approx. $335 million.

Globalstar had an EBITDA margin in the latest quarter approx. 50%. At this margin, estimated EBITDA in 2026 would be $168 million. Since I believe most of the revenues will be high-margin, subscription-based, an EV/EBITDA multiple of 30x seems reasonable. In this case, fair value would be $5.04 billion.

As I explained in the previous article, Band n53 should be worth about $6 billion. If we sum both parts, total valuation should be $11 billion. Globalstar's current market cap is $7.6 billion.

Additionally, I'd like to point out the company has a strong balance sheet. They have $401 million in current assets, while total liabilities stand at approx. $1.8 billion. I believe this is more than enough cash for further growth, especially considering that Apple refunds 95% of the infrastructure costs.

Risks

I believe the main risk is the delay in the launch of the C3 satellite constellation. Since GSAT outsources both manufacturing and launching to MDA Space, Rocket Lab, and SpaceX, any unexpected issues in their supply chain or technical issues will negatively impact Globalstar. Any delays will negatively impact the stock price.

Companies like Iridium (IRDM) are aggressively trying to expand in the IoT space. Still, I believe the sector is large, and with AI and automation, it is poised to further grow, supporting multiple winners. Additionally, as discussed in the article, Globalstar recently announced new contracts in the XCOM RAN vertical, and the IoT hardware revenues rose 60% YoY. This is concrete evidence that Globalstar is executing well.

Plan

Even though I am very bullish over the long term, I have trimmed my position to lock in profits. With the stock rising from $20 in mid-2025 to the current price of around $60, I believe we might see some pullback to the $45-$50 range. In my opinion, some pullback would be healthy, but the stock might also continue to rise. As I emphasized throughout the article, the stock remains undervalued in the long term. If a pullback occurs, I plan to accumulate shares to restore my full position.

data.title: Walmart To Lead Group Of 11 Companies Announcing Annual Dividend Increases In Second Half Of February
data.description:
This is the latest in my series of articles where I provide predictions of annual dividend increases for long-term dividend growth companies. At the end of January, I provided predictions for 19 dividend growth companies that have historically announced annual payout increases in the first half of February. In this article I’ll look at another 11 dividend growth companies that I expect will announce their annual dividend increases in the second half of February.

Before we see how my predictions from the first half of February fared (you can see the original predictions here) and my predictions for the second half of February, there were two companies that I missed:

- Paint company Sherwin-Williams (SHW) announced that it will recommend a 1.3% dividend increase to an annualized $3.20, giving the company a forward yield of 0.87%. If approved, this will be the company’s 48th year of dividend growth.

- Data center REIT Prologis (PLD) announced a 5.9% dividend boost for its 13th year of dividend growth. The new annual dividend of $4.28 gives the company a forward yield of 3.08%.

And here’s one honorable mention: while not technically a dividend growth stock (it skipped its increase last year), The Hershey Company (HSY) announced a 6.0% increase to an annualized $5.808. The increase, its 17th in the last 16 years, gives the candy company a forward yield of 2.61%. The company has paid dividends since at least 1930. I recommended the stock for income investors in a recent technical analysis article. You can see that article here.

(All yields are based on stock prices at the market close on Friday, February 13th.)

Results for Dividend Increase Announcements in the First Half of February

Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI)—23 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 10.1–12.1% increase to $4.36–$4.44

Actual: Deferred until the 2nd half of February

The semiconductor company should announce its next annual dividend boost when it reports earnings on February 17th.

Archer-Daniels-Midland Company (ADM)—51 years

Prediction: 1.0–2.9% increase to $2.06–$2.10

Actual: 2.0% increase to $2.08

Forward yield: 2.99%

As expected, the agricultural supplier announced another 4-cent annual dividend increase.

Allegion plc (ALLE)—13 years

Prediction: 6.9–9.8% increase to $2.18–$2.24

Actual: 7.8% increase to $2.20

Forward yield: 1.23%

Dividend growth at the security company accelerated from last year, but the announcement is still below the 5-year dividend growth rate of 11%.

Avista Corporation (AVA)—24 years

Prediction: 4.1–5.1% increase to $2.04–$2.06

Actual: 0.5% increase to $1.97

Forward yield: 4.57%

The electric and natural gas utility extends its dividend growth streak with a penny-per-share annual increase.

Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO)—16 years

Prediction: 2.4–4.9% increase to $1.68–$1.72

Actual: 2.4% increase to $1.68

Forward yield: 2.19%

The networking company continues to focus on share buybacks, having repurchased $1.4 billion in stock in the most recent quarter. Dividend growth is taking a back seat.

Quest Diagnostics Incorporated (DGX) – 16 years

Prediction: 5.6–6.9% increase to $3.38–$3.42

Actual: 7.5% increase to $3.44

Forward yield: 1.66%

Dividend growth at the medical diagnostics and testing company accelerated slightly from last year’s increase.

Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD)—11 years

Prediction: 5.1–7.6% increase to $3.32–$3.40

Actual: 3.8% increase to $3.28

Forward yield: 2.12%

Biopharmaceutical company Gilead breaks its streak of 8-cent annual increases with this year’s 12-cent boost.

Genuine Parts Company (GPC)—69 years

Prediction: 1.9–2.9% increase to $4.20–$4.24

Actual: Deferred until late February

The auto parts company will announce its 70th year of dividend growth when it announces earnings results before the market opens on Tuesday, February 17th.

ITT Inc. (ITT)—14 years

Prediction: 4.0–6.0% increase to $1.46–$1.49

Actual: 10.3% increase to $1.544

Forward yield: 0.76%

The industrial engineering manufacturer returned to double-digit dividend growth after last year’s 1% increase.

Jack Henry & Associates, Inc. (JKHY)—36 years

Prediction: 5.2–6.9% increase to $2.44–$2.48

Actual: 5.2% increase to $2.44

Forward yield: 1.56%

It’s another year of 5% dividend growth for the financial technology company.

The Coca-Cola Company (KO)—63 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 2.9–3.9% increase to $2.10–$2.12

Actual: Deferred until the 2nd half of February

Investors will have to wait until late February for Coca-Cola’s next dividend increase.

NextEra Energy, Inc. (NEE)—32 years

Prediction: 6.6–9.3% increase to $2.42–$2.48

Actual: 10.0% increase to $2.4928

Forward yield: 2.66%

Florida-based utility NextEra announced its second year of 10% dividend growth. The company also announced its intention to grow the dividend at 6% through 2028.

NorthWestern Energy Group (NWE)—22 years

Prediction: 0–1.5% increase to $2.64–$2.68

Actual: 1.5% increase to $2.68

Forward yield: 3.85%

The electric utility announces its 6th year of 4-cent annual dividend growth as it prepares to merge with Black Hills Corporation.

Public Service Enterprise Group Incorporated (PEG)—14 years

Prediction: 4.8–5.6% increase to $2.64–$2.66

Actual: Deferred until the 2nd half of February

Investors will have to wait until the week of the President’s Day holiday for the next increase from the electric utility.

PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP)—54 years

Prediction: 3.7–5.1% increase to $5.90–$5.98

Actual: 4.0% increase to $5.92

Forward yield: 3.57%

The soft drink and snack company continues to increase its payout by mid-single-digit percentages.

Prudential Financial, Inc. (PRU)—18 years

Prediction: 3.7–4.4% increase to $5.60–$5.64

Actual: 3.7% increase to $5.60

Forward yield: 5.35%

While announcing another 4% payout boost, the insurer also increased its share repurchase authorization by $1 billion.

Restaurant Brands International Inc. (QSR)—13 years

Prediction: 3.2–6.5% increase to $2.56–$2.64

Actual: 4.8% increase to $2.60

Forward yield: 3.93%

Dividend growth at the owner of the Burger King, Popeye’s, Firehouse Subs, and Tim Hortons fast food chains continues to slow, down from last year’s 7% increase.

T. Rowe Price Group, Inc. (TROW)—40 years

Prediction: 4.7–5.5% increase to $5.32–$5.36

Actual: 2.4% increase to $5.20

Forward yield: 5.55%

With another year of 2% growth, dividend increases at the asset manager are lagging the company’s historic growth rate.

Tractor Supply Company (TSCO)—17 years

Prediction: 4.3–8.7% increase to $0.96–$1.00

Actual: 4.3% increase to $0.96

Forward yield: 1.74%

The supplier of outdoor products continues to focus on share buybacks, not dividend growth.

United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS)—16 years

Prediction: 0–0.6% increase to $6.56–$6.60

Actual: 0% increase to $6.56

Forward yield: 5.50%

With earnings continuing to fall, UPS deferred its dividend increase this year. It has until the end of 2026 to grow year-over-year dividends and maintain its status as a dividend growth stock.

Xylem Inc. (XYL)—15 years

Prediction: 11.3–13.8% increase to $1.78–$1.82

Actual: 7.5% increase to $1.72

Forward yield: 1.34%

Dividend growth at the water technologies company slowed from last year’s 11% increase.

Predictions for Dividend Increases in the Second Half of February

There are 11 long-term dividend growth companies that I expect to announce annual increases in the second half of February. First, here is my prediction for one featured company:

Walmart Inc. (WMT)—52 years of dividend growth

The mega-retailer has three reportable segments: Walmart U.S., which covers the company’s domestic operations; Walmart International, which covers Walmart’s operations in 18 countries; and Sam’s Club U.S., which covers the company’s warehouse sales. The three segments represent about 69%, 18%, and 13% of total company sales, respectively. All three segments continue to grow nicely, although International segment earnings were impacted last year by about 4 percentage points due to currency effects.

Walmart continues to make major investments in online retailing and is transitioning its brand to one tied to AI and technology. As part of that shift, Walmart transferred the exchange where its stock is traded from the NYSE to the NASDAQ in November. The investments in technology have powered revenue and earnings growth. Earnings accelerated over the last two years, up 70% from $1.42 a share in fiscal 2023 (ended January 31, 2023) to $2.41 in fiscal year 2025 (ended January 31, 2025). As a result, Walmart’s board has been able to ramp up the dividend growth. After many years of 2% annual dividend growth, in 2024 the company boosted its dividend by 9% and then followed up with a 13% increase last year for a net increase of 23% over the last two years. That 13% dividend boost in 2025 was on EPS growth of 26%. However, EPS growth is expected to be lower this year, which should cause dividend growth to slow. Walmart raised its full-year guidance for fiscal year 2026, but the company is looking at EPS growth of only about 4% when it reports on February 19th. The company’s payout ratio of 35% means that there’s plenty of room for another nice increase, but with the lower earnings growth, I expect we’ll see a dividend boost in the high-single-digit percentages, like the increase in 2024.

Prediction: 6.4–9.6% increase to $1.00–$1.03

Predicted Forward Yield: 0.75–0.77%

And here are my predictions for 10 other long-term dividend growth companies that should announce annual increases in the second half of February:

Company # Yrs Industry Prediction (%) New Annual Rate
Best Buy Co., Inc. (BBY) 22 Consumer Cyclical - Specialty Retail 4.2%–5.8% $3.96 - $4.02
The electronics retailer is seeing faster growth from its international segment, a trend that should continue as the dollar falls against other currencies. Unfortunately for investors, more than 90% of Best Buy’s revenues and profits come from the domestic business, which will limit the positive impact of the international growth. Adjusted EPS growth was flat in 2024, and Best Buy is expecting to report another year of zero growth in 2025. So while the company has a 5-year dividend growth rate of 12%, I think that investors will see mid-single-digit percentage dividend growth, a little lower than last year’s 6% boost. Predicted Forward Yield: 6.02 - 6.11%
Essex Property Trust (ESS) 31 REIT - Residential 3.9%–4.7% $10.68 - $10.76
Essex Property owns more than 250 apartment communities with more than 62,000 apartments in Southern and Northern California and the Seattle Metro area. The company’s business growth has slowed quite a bit; core funds from operations (a metric used by REITs, instead of income, that accounts for the tax benefits of real estate ownership) were up only 2% in 2025 and are expected to be flat in 2026. While this type of growth won’t support continued dividend growth in the mid-single-digit percentages, I think that the company has enough liquidity and cash flow to maintain the dividend growth trend. Investors should see a boost slightly below last year’s 5% increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 4.25–4.28%
Eaton Corporation plc (ETN) 16 Industrials - Specialty Industrial Machinery 10.1% - 12.5% $4.58 - $4.68
Eaton is a power management company that manufactures products for a variety of industries, including data centers, utilities, machine building, and aerospace businesses. The company had growth across the board in 2025 but particularly in the electrical and aerospace segments, which drove a 12% increase in adjusted EPS. The company’s also provided guidance of another 10% in 2026. This type of earnings growth will support another good dividend boost. I’m expecting a boost in the 10% range, similar to last year’s 11% increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.18 - 1.20%
The Home Depot, Inc. (HD) 16 Consumer Cyclical - Home Improvement Retail 2.0% - 3.0% $9.38 - $9.48
After consistently growing earnings, the home improvement retailer has struggled over the last couple of years with flat earnings. This has impacted dividend growth—despite being known as a fast dividend grower, with a 5-year compounded growth average of 10%, last year’s boost was a mere 2%. Earnings growth continues to stall, with the company expecting to announce a 5% drop in adjusted EPS when it reports earnings on February 24th. Home Depot’s also been using its free cash flow to repurchase shares, dropping share count by 8% since 2020. With the drop in EPS, I expect the company to announce another small increase. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.40 - 2.42%
Linde plc (LIN) 32 Basic Materials - Specialty Chemicals 6.7%–8.0% $6.40 - $6.48
Industrial gases company Linde continues to see good earnings growth, with 2025’s EPS growth of 6% the 6th straight year of earnings growth. The company has compounded its EPS by 23% since 2019, with EPS running from $4.19 in 2019 to last year’s $14.61. The company applied its growing earnings to both boosting its payout at about 8–9% annually, along with buying back more than 12% of outstanding stock since 2019. With this year’s growth rate along with expectations of another 7% growth this year, investors can expect a dividend boost slightly below the previous payout growth rate. I expect an increase in the 6–8% range. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.33 - 1.35%
McGrath RentCorp (MGRC) 33 Industrials - Rental & Leasing Services 3.3% - 4.7% $6.20 - $6.28
The business-to-business rental company provides modular offices and storage solutions, along with electronic test equipment. After an outstanding 2024, with revenues up 10% and EPS more than doubling, things are slowing down in 2025. The company provided full-year guidance of EPS growth of 1% and revenue growth of 3% for 2025. With the excellent income growth last year, investors saw an above-average 8% boost. With income growth slowing down this year, I expect the company to announce an increase closer to the 5-year growth average of 4%. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.70–1.72%
Old Republic International Corporation (ORI) 44 Insurance - Property & Casualty 5.2% - 6.9% $1.20 - $1.24
The insurance company generally announces dividend boosts in the mid-single-digit percentages and frequently rewards shareholders with special dividends. The most recent special dividend was a $2.50 per share payout just last month—this is the 5th special dividend in the last 6 years. Company revenues, excluding investment gains, were flat, but Old Republic still posted 4% EPS growth. While the company announced a 9% increase last year, I expect this year’s increase to be in the more traditional range of 5–7%; any additional profits will likely be paid out late this year or early next year. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.82 - 2.91%
Silgan Holdings Inc. (SLGN) 21 Consumer Cyclical - Packaging & Containers 5.0% $0.84
Dividend growth at the manufacturer of rigid packaging and metal containers has slowed over the last two years as the company shifted from 8-cent annual increases to 4-cent annual increases over each of the last two years. Silgan isn’t likely to accelerate payout growth, however, as EPS growth isn’t able to support larger increases. Adjusted EPS in 2026 is expected to be about 2%, after coming in with 3% growth in 2025. While eventually the company won’t be able to support 4-cent annual growth forever, the current payout ratio of 22% leaves room for another increase at that level. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.73%
Sempra (SRE) 21 Utilities - Diversified 2.3%–3.9% $2.64 - $2.68
With 40 million customers across Southern California and Texas, Sempra is one of the largest electric and natural gas utilities in the United States. The company sports a 10-year dividend growth rate of about 6.5%, slightly lower than its long-term EPS growth guidance of 7–9%. However, the company’s looking at a drop in both EPS and adjusted EPS in 2025. And while both are expected to rebound in 2026, investors will have to wait until then for a return to historical dividend growth. I expect the company to announce a boost slightly below last year’s 4% boost. Predicted Forward Yield: 2.79 - 2.83%
Steel Dynamics, Inc. (STLD) 14 Basic Materials - Steel 8.0% - 11.0% $2.16 - $2.22
After many years of minimal dividend growth in the 2010s, payout growth at the steel company exploded beginning in 2017. That was followed up with massive EPS growth beginning in 2020, peaking in 2022 at $20.92 per share. Since then, earnings have slowly decreased, with EPS falling 19% in 2025 to $7.99. Dividend increases have also been supported by an aggressive stock buyback program, as the company has retired more than 30% of outstanding shares since 2019, including 4% of shares in 2025. Annual dividend increases from the company have swung wildly over the years, from the mid-single digits in the mid-2010s to a peak of 30% in 2022. Still, with a payout ratio of 25%, there’s still plenty of headroom for Steel Dynamics to announce a dividend increase close to or even over 10%. Predicted Forward Yield: 1.13 - 1.16%

Summary

As I’ve seen in previous years, there was a flood of dividend increases in the first half of February as companies assessed the results of the previous year and made their decisions on how much to boost their payout. Notable increases in the first half of February include 10% boosts from Florida utility NextEra Energy and industrial component manufacturer ITT. Most other increases were in the low-to-middle single-digit percentages, while shipping company UPS, with falling earnings, skipped its dividend increase.

The latter half of February will bring fewer dividend increases, although investors in retail giant Walmart can look forward to a 53rd year of payout growth. I expect we’ll see a double-digit percentage increase from industrial machinery conglomerate Eaton and a nice increase from metals company Steel Dynamics. And we’ll see Dividend Kings Genuine Parts and Coca-Cola announce their next annual increase, with Genuine Parts extending its streak to 70 years.

data.title: 5 Closed-End Fund Buys In The Month Of January 2026
data.description:
To kick off the first month of the new year, we have micro- and small-cap stocks surging, followed by mid-caps also having a strong first month. The major indexes, Nasdaq 100, S&P 500, and Dow Jones Industrial Average, weren't participating to the same extent, but they did start off on a positive note. Though thanks to earnings season, we have seen quite a few more volatile days as the mega-caps post their latest quarterly results.

In terms of sectors, we have energy surging, followed by strong moves in basic materials, consumer staples, and industrials.

With that, every month I make some purchases in my closed-end fund portfolio. These five are the names that I kicked off 2026 with, which are all positions I held previously, so this was simply adding to them.

#1 Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) and #2 Cohen & Steers Real Estate Opps and Income Fund (RLTY)

I'm lumping both RQI and RLTY into the same basket because these funds have significant overlap. We covered RQI more specifically quite recently. For RLTY, our last update was quite a while ago, but it can still be worth giving a look at because some of the points remain relevant. This is because we discuss the impact of rates more broadly and look back at the performance relative to its sister funds.

With that, I also picked these names up at the beginning of the month, which is important to note because at that time, RQI was trading at an attractive discount. Since then, the discount on RQI has tightened up quite meaningfully.

While I believe that RQI is a solid hold over the long term, I wouldn't be buying now. At least, not in an aggressive manner. For RLTY, the discount, relatively speaking, is looking more appealing, but looking back over the longer term, even this discount isn't necessarily a screaming buy.

RLTY is the much younger fund, coming to market near the start of 2022. These two funds have some significant overlap in their portfolio, which includes many of the same top holdings. That said, more broadly speaking, the split for RLTY's portfolio is 70% common equity positions, and then the other 30% is preferred and fixed income. For RQI, the fund last listed its allocation at an 82/18% split, respectively.

RLTY's current distribution rate comes out to 8.79%, with a NAV rate of 8.30%. RQI recently paid out a special and increased its regular monthly distribution, bumping it up from $0.08 to $0.09. In our previous write-up, we discussed in more depth why that likely occurred. To summarize briefly, they are sitting on a sizeable allocation of realized capital gains. As a regulated investment company, they have distribution requirements where almost all of their income and gains must be paid out. The current distribution rate for RQI comes in at 8.84%.

#3 Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (ETW)

Another fund that I covered quite recently, ETW, provides exposure to a globally diversified portfolio. The fund was trading at a double-digit discount to its net asset value, representing a historically relatively wide discount level. Though that is relative to the longer term. In the last several years, a deep discount has been fairly normal.

This goes back to when they reduced their distribution in 2022, which sent the fund from a premium to a discount level. On the other hand, the fund also increased its payout at the end of 2024. The current distribution works out to 8.49%, but with its substantial discount, the NAV rate comes in at a more modest 7.60%.

It is worthwhile to note again that this fund, along with a couple of other Eaton Vance funds, has somewhat recently switched their target overwrite. At one point, ETW overwrote nearly 100% of the notional value of its portfolio. This has been reduced to less than 50% now.

This is relevant because, as a call writing fund, the amount a fund is overwritten can cap upside during bull markets. The flip side of this is that the more the fund overwrites, the more it can also collect in terms of option premiums. That can be helpful in a flat or downward market. So with this change, they would be betting on the fact of more capital appreciation potential in the future to help fund their payout.

#4 Cohen & Steers Tax-Advantaged Preferred Securities and Income Fund (PTA)

PTA is another name that I covered very recently in a full write-up. The fund recently started to see its discount open up substantially. It didn't get down to the levels we saw in 2022/2023, when interest rates were putting substantial pressure on the fund.

As a preferred and income-focused fund, they are highly sensitive to changes in long-term risk-free U.S. Treasury Rates. They also employ leverage through borrowings, which means they are impacted by short-term rates as well. Fortunately, similar to the other CNS funds, they have most of their leverage fixed through interest rate swaps.

At the same time, it isn't entirely hedged, and as the Fed isn't expected to cut rates back to zero, they can still see their financing costs rise in the future. For example, the variable rate listed above is nearly 5%, but their weighted average rate on fixed financing comes in at a much more modest 2.4%. So this remains a headwind, but every time the Fed cuts, that headwind is reduced.

#5 Oxford Lane Capital Corporation (OXLC)

The final buy of the month came in as OXLC. This was on the back of more troubling news, a 50% distribution cut and a massive drop in NAV. On September 30, 2025, NAV stood at $19.19, and they updated that the December 31, 2025, NAV stands at $15.51. That made the ~15% drop quite appropriate the day that was announced, and the discount remains significant on this name.

A name that used to benefit heavily from trading at a substantial premium regularly. That premium benefited them as they could sell shares in an accretive manner, which would have otherwise slowed the NAV erosion the fund experiences over time.

To reiterate and for some background on this position, here is a recap from my previous monthly buy piece, where I purchased OXLC last.

With all that said, as I've touched on in a prior update, I've been averaging down into this position. This was after I sold earlier in the year.

...as [Eagle Point Credit Co (ECC)] ECC and OXLC have been on a strong run for several years now. When volatility increases during economic downturns, these funds get hit quite hard due to their added leverage on an already volatile asset. So it's a bit like musical chairs, you need to be prepared and try not to be the last one standing.

Before getting back into ECC or OXLC, I'll likely be waiting for the distribution cuts. With these funds, it is generally a matter of when and not if, due to their volatile nature. That said, that "when" may be in a few months or in a few years. Though investors likely take comfort in knowing that OXLC has declared its same monthly distribution out through September.

As the fund cut its distribution and announced a reverse split, that's when I started to enter back in.

OXLC was beaten down in July on the back of a distribution reduction and reverse split announcement. That prompted me to pick up a position during July, but the weakness continued into August, hitting new all-time lows, which I then added even further to the position. Since then, it has rebounded a bit, but over the long term, we should expect erosion to continue.

That said, there appear to be periods of 3-5 years when you can hold this name. One of the problems is knowing exactly when the bottom is in during these volatile periods, which is why I took an approach of investing in smaller portions as opposed to initially picking up such a large position in July. At least for now, that seems like it worked out as we hit the new lows.

So the big hits keep coming for this name, and this latest buy was to average down. Fortunately, I sold earlier last year, but then, unfortunately, I bought back in too quickly as I followed through with a buy after the initial cut.

With this latest cut, it is a much more substantial cut than what they had done previously, and so I added more aggressively. This larger cut is something more than I would have expected or even wanted to see initially—which is exactly what I mentioned when initially picking up the position again.

In fact, I believe that their ~11% distribution reduction was being too conservative and trying to ease the market into the payout cuts. I think that they probably have further to cut in the future or face ever-accelerating NAV erosion. Therefore, I only reinitiated a rather small position and will see how it goes from here before adding anything additional.

This is a more speculative position, and I keep my overall CLO exposure relatively small compared to my portfolio. Even after this latest buying, OXLC, Eagle Point Income Company (EIC), and XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT) account for ~1.5% of my portfolio. This doesn't include the preferreds issued by these CLO equity CEFs.

data.title: Howmet Aerospace: I Was Wrong, But The Stock Remains Expensive
data.description:
Howmet Aerospace (NYSE:HWM), a key supplier to the aerospace and defense industry, surged 24% since my last sell-rated report outperforming the flat S&P 500. The sell rating has obviously not worked out, and with that in mind, I'm reviewing the valuation framework along with an update on the most recent earnings, outlining that even against a premium multiple, the stock remains expensive.

Howmet Aerospace Sees Broad-Based Turbine Strength

Fourth quarter sales grew 15% to $2.2 billion. Commercial Aerospace sales grew 13% driven by engine spares and production rate plans at original equipment manufacturers. Defense sales grew 20% driven by engine spares which were up 32% and the F-35 program with F-35 spared demand exceeding demand from OEMs.

Commercial transportation sales grew 4% driven by lower volumes in the wheels business partially, but with strength due to favorable currency effects and tariff pass-through.

Gas turbine sales increased 32% driven by increasing demand for turbines to power data centers and electricity generation.

As total revenues grew 15% to $2.2 billion, we see that adjusted EBITDA grew 29% to $653 million, with margins expanding 3.3 points to 30.1%, driven by strong spare parts mix, volumes, and leveraging automation. Adjusted operating income grew 34% to $580 million as margins expanded 3.8 points to 26.8%, while adjusted earnings per share increased 42% to $1.05.

Discussion of Howmet Aerospace Segment Results

As a reminder, Howmet Aerospace does not present its results by end market. Instead, it reports through four segments:

  1. Engine Products
  2. Fastening Systems
  3. Engineered Structures
  4. Forged Wheels

Engine Product sales increased 20% year-on-year to $1.16 billion. Commercial Aerospace sales increased 17% while Defense sales improved 18% while gas turbine sales grew 32%. The increase was driven by aftermarket demand in the commercial aerospace end market, F-35 in defense along with strong spares demand and data center demand driving gas turbine sales. EBITDA grew 31% to $396 million, and like in prior quarters, this includes a net head count addition to continue scaling the business.

Fastening Systems sales increased 13% to $454 million driven by 20% growth in commercial aerospace on narrow body production recovery and higher value fasteners driving a favorable mix. Widebody recovery remains sluggish, which actually means that the strong sales increase still largely is without substantial widebody driven sales. Defense aerospace sales increased 7% driven by broader defense demand. We also see that industrial demand is now explicitly called out as a growth driver during the quarter driven by broader fastener applications and renewable energy demand driving 14% in sales. The only weak spot unsurprisingly is commercial transportation, which is experiencing headwind from tariffs and macro. Adjusted EBITDA increased 25% to $139 million.

Engineered Structures sales were up 4% to $287 million. Commercial Aerospace revenues declined 6% driven by Howmet Aerospace exiting lower margin work. So, this is a controlled shift bringing a decline in sales. Defense Aerospace sales were up 37% as destocking on the F-35 program ended reviving demand. Adjusted EBITDA increased 24% driving up margins 3.5 points to 22% and that shows the rationalization of the commercial aerospace structures business now focusing on higher margin work and the F-35 revival.

Forged Wheels revenues increased 9% to $264 million despite a 10% reduction in volumes. The company is passing through higher input and tariff related costs along with a stronger product mix during the quarter. EBITDA grew 20% to $79 million. Forged Wheels is not the most impressive part of Howmet Aerospace, but we note that in a very challenging business environment cost flexing and headcount rationalization is still driving EBITDA and EBITDA margin improvements.

Howmet Aerospace Guidance Has Additional Upside

Howmet Aerospace had previously guided for around $9 billion in sales, and we now see that the company actually guided for $9.1 billion plus or minus $100 million. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be between $2.71-$2.81 billion, indicating margins between 30.1% and 30.5%, indicating a 1-point expansion at the mid-point. Adjusted earnings per share are guided at $4.35-$4.55 with free cash flow of $1.6 billion at the mid-point. The guidance shows broad-based demand in aftermarket, OEM and datacenter and power generation.

This excludes the acquisition of CAM expected to drive $485-$495 million in EBITDA on an annualized basis with substantial opportunities to develop margins in the years ahead.

For the first quarter, the company expects $2.225-$2.245 billion in sales and 30.6%-30.7% margins with $1.09-$1.11 in earnings per share.

Howmet Aerospace Is Fairly Valued

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

In my prior assessment, I rated Howmet Aerospace a sell. I'm now upgrading that to hold as the sell rating my have been too harsh. Changes made to the assessment are the following:

  • CAM EBITDA, sales and free cash flow have been added for 2026-2028.
  • The EV/EBITDA multiple for 2024 has been used. This 29.1x EV/EBITDA multiple is substantially higher than the multiple of the peer group and the company median multiple.
  • Redemption of preferred shares has been implemented.

What we see is that Howmet Aerospace is not necessarily unattractive, but in the near term there's no upside and the stock is fairly valued. So, we need a premium trading multiple for a fair valuation. The only thing that would make the stock attractive is if the company can show substantial operating leverage that would drive margins and cash flow combined with strong double-digit growth stretching to and beyond the end of the decade.

Howmet Aerospace is not unattractive if we look at the financial expectations. Margins are set to saturate around 31% unless the company substantially increase operating leverage. Cash conversion is looking good and we also note that if the acquisition does not bring a lot of capex, the capital intensity will improve. I do expect that the company will require to raise debt to finance the acquisition of CAM. With continued shareholder returns we still see that the net debt level will decline so over the longer-term I don’t think there is any major pressure for Howmet driven by its debt levels, but the fundamentals simply do not support a lot of upside in the stock price.

Conclusion: Howmet Aerospace Remains Expensive

Howmet’s financial performance is impressive. At the same time, we note that the stock is expensive and some consolidation in the stock price is required. At current prices, there simply is not a lot of attractive upside left. We have to look at 2028 against an elevated trading multiple before we see upside and at current prices the stock is fairly valued. So, I'm upgrading the stock to hold, but my view remains that the price remains too high for an attractive investment case.

data.title: Duolingo: The Market Is Misunderstanding The "SaaSpocalypse"
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past few months, SaaS companies have taken a massive hit due to Anthropic releasing AI agents that can be incorporated into software. Duolingo, Inc. (DUOL) has been included in this selloff and has decreased 33% over the past month.

I think part of this selloff has to do with the fear that AI will make Duolingo obsolete, which has been the recurring fear since the start of its decline. But most recently, the “SaaSpocalypse” is the fear that SaaS' pricing models will change due to AI agents using it instead of humans, reducing the predictability of its cashflows. As a software company with recurring revenue and a centralized platform, it makes sense to categorize Duolingo as SaaS. Being that Duolingo earns most of its revenue from subscriptions, this recurring revenue, along with its massive growth, has caused Duolingo to trade at a premium, similar to other SaaS companies.

In this article I will argue that, in fact, Duolingo will not be affected by AI agents, should not be considered part of the SaaSpocalypse, and that the market is misunderstanding the impact of AI.

The SaaSpocalypse situation

SaaS is a business model in which software is delivered over the internet and users pay a subscription per seat per month. The use of seats per month is the perfect pricing model for SaaS, as multiple developers under one corporation can use the software. It also allows SaaS companies and investors to have better projections of future cash flows using ARR as a metric.

This is all standard information, but it is important to explain how AI agents can affect this. Anthropic has released AI agents that assist the users with increasingly complex tasks in specific software. Moats that SaaS has enjoyed, such as high switching costs, integration complexity, and network effect, have all been threatened by AI agents. As explained on Semianalysis:

“A simplistic example is an agent can now query a Postgres database directly on your behalf, generate a chart, and email it to a stakeholder. That was effectively the cost of a SaaS workflow like Salesforce (CRM), and it doesn’t need to train humans on UI changes or update software.”

AI agents can migrate data automatically and dramatically reduce switching costs. Corporations do not need to train humans to interact with a complex UI when an agent can do it for them. I now use Claude for Excel on a daily basis to create tables, calculate ratios, and organize models. Eventually, AI agents may replace many interactions humans have with software, so the question is, should SaaS companies charge the same for AI agents as they do for humans?

Because AI agents are now interacting with software, it is possible for multiple people to use the same agent under a single subscription. This essentially means that the predictability that ARR and seat-based pricing provide is now challenged for the first time. Pricing will be forced to move to a per-usage model. SaaS may no longer have the necessary moats for its companies to enjoy high margins and profitability metrics, and investors cannot model predictable cash flows.

Where does Duolingo Fit Into This?

Duolingo, despite being considered a SaaS, is not similar to the SaaS companies threatened by AI agents. Duolingo is not an application corporations use to create projects like the SaaS that AI agents are targeting. It is not a collaborative productivity platform for corporations and offices like Microsoft 365. It is not CAD software used by every engineering and architectural firm to design large structures or infrastructure projects like AutoCAD. Duolingo does not charge per seat, and the sole objective is for users to learn in the most practical way. Duolingo does not have the same moats as a typical SaaS, it does not have a complex UI that requires training to use it, it does not have high switching costs, and there is no output file.

I recommend checking my previous analysis on Duolingo, as I go in depth on the moats that it has. Duolingo’s moat has more to do with the data dominance that it has among learning apps and how it has been perfected to be as addictive as social media. Simple features like streaks, progress roads, and competitive activities make Duolingo one of the most engaging apps, comparable to social media. AI has allowed Duolingo to double the number of courses it has in less than a year. It has also allowed it to introduce a new feature called Max, which allows users to practice a language by speaking to an AI. AI has been a helping hand to Duolingo, and no pricing model is at risk.

One bearish argument that I have seen around is that AI now has the ability to create a language learning app similar to Duolingo. This is partially true. While AI still has a long way to go, in the future it will be able to perfectly replicate every single feature that Duolingo has. But, again, Duolingo has been optimized to be as addictive as social media, and the app's ability to adapt to one's learning style is what I think will make Duolingo not go anywhere. A replica would not have collected years of a user's learning curve. No learning institution has the amount of data Duolingo has, and, as adoption increases, it has the potential to become a digital learning tool for formal education.

Risks

With this bullish thesis, it is important to lay down the risks.

Despite declining 80% since its peak and 43% after my article, Duolingo is still at a high valuation with more room to keep falling.

Two of the downward forces that also contributed to Duolingo’s fall are still affecting its price. First of all, the controversy regarding AI adoption still has had an effect on its largely successful marketing efforts. Social Blade has reported a collapse in its monthly gained followers and likes on TikTok and other social media. You can see its TikTok numbers below:

This means that Duolingo is not at full throttle with gaining new users which was one of the objectives laid out by management in its Q3 FY25 shareholder letter:

“In Q3 we decided to shift the balance towards longer-term initiatives. In particular, we’re investing proportionally more in teaching better, and we’re prioritizing user growth over monetization in the A/B tests that get launched. This shift is reflected in our full-year guidance”

This takes us into the second effect still in play. Wall Street does not like when a company diverges away from monetization. This is a long term shift that will continue to make Wall Street wary until they see monetary results.

Conclusion

Despite a 43% decline since I gave DUOL a Strong Buy, I still believe that the market is getting this wrong and is overlooking the opportunities it has. It has been a long and painful journey since peaking at $540 in May. I was initially interested in this stock because I believed that its AI controversy was misunderstood and saw it as just a short-term headwind. I was then more interested when another bearish thesis emerged, centered on the introduction of AI translators and AI building apps. I believed again that Duolingo was misunderstood and its opportunities were overlooked. Now, Duolingo’s inclusion in the SaaSpocalypse narrative reinforced my view that it is misunderstood. Despite the risks that I laid out, I think this is a big opportunity to buy a stock after three bearish theses have driven its price down by 80%. As said in my previous analysis, this stock has room to keep falling, and it will probably continue to have wild swings. If you are financially and psychologically able to tolerate this level of risk, I maintain a Strong Buy recommendation.

data.title: Roku's Razor And Blade Model Drives Long-Term Profitable Growth Prospects
data.description:
I previously covered Roku, Inc. (ROKU) in December 2025, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating then, thanks to the recent bounce off the established Q4'25 support levels of $90s, the robust profitable growth trends as observed in the promising FQ4 '25 guidance, and the likely to be cheaper valuations moving forward.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the ROKU stock here, thanks to their razor-blade model through the Device segment already driving an excellent user acquisition trend and, consequently, driving the robust Platform monetization across subscription/advertising.

This is significantly aided by the promising FY2026 guidance, the notably richer balance sheet, and the compelling valuations.

ROKU Delivers Robust Razor-Blade Model Monetization Cadence

Since my last buy rating, ROKU has been rather volatile, as the stock has lost -28.9% of its value since the 52 weeks high.

This was potentially attributed to the prior "tech rout" in January 2026, as "investors are becoming increasingly worried that advances in artificial intelligence, including the growing coding capabilities of Anthropic’s Claude, will leave many software products and services obsolete," with ROKU seemingly correcting in sympathy as with many of its streaming peers in varying degrees.

Well, this is before the market cheers up at ROKU's outsized FQ4'25 performance and promising FY2026 guidance, with it triggering a pre-market jump by +15.8% at the time of writing.

This rally is unsurprising indeed, as ROKU's Platform segment consistently drives their improving business operations despite the yet profitable Devices segment.

The same has been observed in their increasingly profitable adj. EBITDA margins of 12.1% in FQ4'25 (+5.6 points YoY) despite the flattish overall gross margins of 43.5% (+0.8 points YoY), significantly aided by the improving operating efficiency with lower expenses by -1.9% YoY.

Combined with the robust Platform revenue growth of +18% YoY in FY2025 despite the tougher comparison from a year ago at +18% YoY, thanks to the improving domestic/global advertising monetization, the growing subscription base, and the increased streaming hours by +15% YoY, it is unsurprising that ROKU has been able to achieve a robust operating leverage thus far.

Much of its tailwinds are attributed to their successful "razor-blade model," with the loss-making/highly accessible devices already "resulting in Roku being used in over half of U.S. broadband households" and the growing Platform segment with global Streaming Households at over 90M in FQ4'25 as the management guides over 100M global Streaming Households in 2026.

Once the users are entrenched in the Roku platform, ROKU then drives improved AI-led content discovery and, consequently, deeper subscriber engagement, with the revamped Roku Experience on its Home Screen already driving "more than half of all (premium) subscription sign-ups."

This is aided by the expanding, vertically integrated advertising capabilities, as the company leverages their Roku Ads Manager, a self-serve platform for small and medium-sized businesses [SMB], and their "platform partnerships such as Spaceback and the availability of low-cost generative AI video tools," allowing advertisers to create and publish advertising campaigns "in under five minutes."

Given that EMarketer already expects the CTV advertising spend to grow from $27B in 2025 to $46.9B in 2028, I am of the opinion that ROKU aggressive razor-blade model is likely to drive an excellent user acquisition trend and, consequently, drive the Platform segment's robust profitable growth trends through subscription/advertising opportunities.

The same has been observed in the management hinting at their "ability to sustain double-digit Platform revenue growth while continuing to expand both operating and net income margins" in 2026 and beyond - with it lending credence to the stock's outsized price performance post FQ4'25 earnings call.

ROKU's Profitable Growth Trend Is Compellingly Valued

Thanks to the accelerating FQ4'25 EPS growth of $0.53 (+320.8% YoY) against the FY2025 EPS performance of $0.59 (+166.2% YoY), it is unsurprising that ROKU has felt confident to offer a promising FY2026 net income guidance of $325M (+267.8% YoY) and my estimated FY2026 EPS of $2.13 (+261% YoY, based on the last reported FQ4'25 shares outstanding of 152.29M).

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates appear to be extremely promising, with the streaming company expected to report a robust bottom-line expansion at a 3Y CAGR of +83.7%.

Thanks to the -28.9% meltdown from the 52-week high, ROKU is a lot more compelling at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.93x and FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 17.63x, down from the 1Y mean of 2.33x/26.32x and the 5Y mean of 4.69x/49.9x, respectively.

When comparing ROKU's 3% streaming market share as of December 2025, FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 17.63x, and the robust FY2025 adj. EBITDA growth of +61.6% YoY to its other top 5 peers by streaming share, including

  • Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) - 12.7% share/16.91x/+17.5% YoY in FY2025 (diversified advertising/cloud/YouTube),
  • Netflix (NFLX) - 9% share/19.28x/+15.5% YoY in FY2025,
  • Disney (DIS) - 4.7%/10.57x/+11% YoY in FY2025 (diversified media/theme park/cruise), and
  • Amazon (AMZN) - 4.3%/10.43x/+20% YoY in FY2025 (diversified commerce/cloud/advertising/Amazon Prime), respectively,

I am of the opinion that ROKU remains cheaply valued here, thanks to its high, double-digit adjusted EBITDA expansion, as similarly observed in the management's promising FY2026 adjusted EBITDA growth guidance of +51% YoY and FY2026 adjusted EBITDA margin guidance of 11.5% (+2.7 points YoY), significantly aided by the recent meltdown.

This is on top of their richer free cash flow conversion rate of 115% in FY2025 (+37 points YoY) along with the management's guidance of "our free cash flow continuing to be strong and outpacing EBITDA beyond this year" and "over $1 billion in free cash flow by the end of 2028" as the company enters an era of profitable growth trend over the next few years.

ROKU Remains A Great Buy

For now, ROKU previously returned much of its prior H2'25 gains after breaching numerous support levels and the 50/100/200 day moving averages, with the prior oversold status directly contributing to the post FQ4'25 earnings call's upward recovery and the retest of the 200 day moving averages.

With the bulls already defending a floor after the prior meltdown, I am of the opinion that the stock is compelling here, significantly aided by the cheaper valuations and the profitable growth trends as observed in the FQ4'25 performance/promising FY2026 guidance.

Combined with the notably healthier balance sheet at a net cash position of $2.3B (+6.4% YoY) with no debt (aside from lease liabilities) and the ongoing share repurchases while "achieving near 0% dilution for Q4," thanks to the richer TTM Free Cash Flow generation at $483.61M (+137.9% YoY), I am reiterating my Buy rating for ROKU.

data.title: Cintas: Near-Perfect Execution Is Already Priced In
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Frank Balestriere as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Thesis

Cintas (CTAS) is one of the most consistent companies I follow. There are few industrials that have executed as well over the last decade, which has allowed the company to reach nearly 23% operating margins.

In December, the company delivered another strong quarter. Revenue increased 9.3%, EPS rose 11%, and management raised full-year guidance. Yet even after that beat-and-raise quarter, the stock remains roughly 11% below its all-time high set back in June.

At 40x forward earnings, the issue is not execution. The problem is the market is already pricing in near-perfect expectations for years to come. While Cintas will likely continue to dominate its industry, the current valuation leaves little room for error. Therefore, I rate Cintas as a Hold, preferring to wait for a better entry point.

Company Overview

With a market cap of nearly $76 billion, Cintas is by far the largest provider of uniform rental facility services and workplace safety solutions to businesses across North America. Serving over 1 million businesses, the company operates through a huge logistics network of 12,100 local delivery routes, 478 operational facilities, and 12 distribution centers.

While asset-heavy, the business model itself is relatively simple. Cintas enters into contracts with its customers to pick up dirty garments and drop off clean ones weekly. This creates a predictable, recurring revenue stream.

Today, Cintas operates in primarily two segments:

  1. Uniform Rental and Facility Services (~78% of Revenue): This segment includes the rental and cleaning of uniforms and other garments as well as related facility services such as restroom supplies, mats, mops, and towels.

  2. First Aid and Safety Services (~11% of Revenue): This is the faster-growing segment, which provides workplace safety products and services including first aid cabinets, eye washing stations, AEDs, and safety training.

The rest of Cintas’s revenue comes from its “All Other” reporting segment (~11%), which offers fire protection services like inspection, testing, and servicing of fire extinguishers, suppression systems, and alarms. This segment also includes its Uniform Direct Sales segment that generates revenue from the outright sale of uniforms and related products, rather than rental.

Evaluating the Impact of Competitive Advantages

To understand Cintas’s premium valuation is to understand the advantages that it has relative to the competitive landscape.

According to IBISWorld and industry trade groups like the TRSA, the U.S. industrial laundry sector is highly fragmented, made up of roughly 1,800 businesses. Cintas’ closest national competitors are Vestis (VSTS) (formerly Aramark Uniforms) and UniFirst (UNF). Beneath these larger companies lie about 500 to 600 independent “mom-and-pop” businesses that specialize directly in uniform rental.

Cintas has successfully pulled away from these competitors, which can be directly credited to a few competitive advantages.

The Cintas Sales Model

Since Cintas operates a route-based service model, it relies on a unique hybrid employee—the truck drivers. But these employees act as more than just truck drivers; they are also service sales representatives (SSRs).

These frontline employees are put through an extensive training program. There they are taught to win new clients and manage relationships with existing customers. Because SSRs interact with clients so often, they develop deeper relationships that position them to identify additional needs. This leads to cross-selling opportunities like restocking first aid cabinets or inspecting fire extinguishers. It also creates the opportunity for Cintas to identify new services needed by customers. For example, in 2018, they introduced WaterBreak, a workplace hydration solution.

Given the importance of these employees, success is dependent on finding and retaining talent. The process of winning new clients can be grueling, generally involving prospecting and cold contacting new businesses. However, the fundamentals suggest the model works. The Uniform Rental and Facilities segment revenue has grown at a CAGR of 6.2% since 2018. The success of cross-selling is most evident in the First Aid and Safety Services segment, which now makes up more than 12% of revenue. Revenues in this segment have grown at about 11% CAGR.

Cross-selling is particularly important because the deeper integration of Cintas’ services creates switching costs. The logic is it is annoying to replace a company that handles uniforms. It is downright disruptive if that business also handles first aid supplies, fire safety, and restroom supplies.

Route Density

As Cintas retains and adds more customers, a route-based business like Cintas’ benefits from having more stops per truck on a given route—referred to as route density. Since the cost to send a truck to a zip code is fixed, each additional stop added to the route increases revenue disproportionately to its incremental cost.

This operating leverage has been a driving force behind the company’s margin expansion over the past decade. Since 2018, Cintas’ revenue has grown at a CAGR of 6.9%, while operating income has grown faster at 13% CAGR, allowing operating margins to expand to around 23% (source: 2018 10-K & 2025 10-K). To put these figures into perspective, Vestis and UniFirst currently have operating margins in the 6-8% range.

A fair question, however, is how much density remains available. Cintas already operates roughly 12,000 routes across North America, and it has been doing business in major metropolitan markets for decades. Many of those routes are likely already mature and densely packed.

Management frequently points to the market opportunity of 16 million “No-Programmers” as evidence of their remaining runway. While this number is significant, many of these businesses are likely smaller businesses where the economics could be less attractive.

Despite this, management remains confident. On the most recent earnings call, they confirmed that incremental margins remain in their long-term “sweet spot” range of 25%–35%, and they see incremental margins in the second half of FY 2026 in the low 30s. This suggests that new revenue is still carrying attractive economics, providing confidence in future margin expansion.

Management continues to pursue route density through both organic growth and M&A. The company has seen success adding new clients in recession-resistant sectors, including healthcare, hospitality, education, and government. The strategy involves offering proprietary solutions to these businesses, not just uniforms. For example, deploying automated scrub-dispensing technology recently allowed them to win an entire 14-hospital network by solving their inventory loss problems.

Acquisitions have also been an important part of the strategy. Over the last 5 years, the company has spent on average about 8% of free cash flow per year on tuck-in deals across all three of their businesses (source: 2021 10-K & 2025 10-K). These acquisitions are specifically focused on buying redundant routes and packing more stops into existing Cintas trucks. Thus far in 2026, the company has spent over $85 million on acquisitions. Given the number of smaller regional businesses, the consolidation runway appears healthy.

Scale and Technology-Driven Efficiencies

Scale has become a significant advantage for Cintas. It has allowed the company to buy textiles, vehicles, and fuel at wholesale rates, creating a tangible cost advantage over its competitors. Also, having a national presence means that they are one of the few options available for national accounts like hotel chains that prefer a single vendor across the country.

This cash flow has been used to fund operational efficiencies, including technology investments. These include auto-sortation systems for laundry facilities and SmartTruck, proprietary software that optimizes routes, reducing fuel consumption and driver idle time.

The myCintas portal serves as a frictionless sales channel. The portal handles billing and allows customers to more easily purchase additional products without waiting for their weekly route driver. While the financial impact of the service is more difficult to quantify, we can see the strategic value of offering customers a “one-stop shop” that increases customer stickiness.

A Note on Pricing

Despite these strengths, one thing to note is Cintas’ lack of pricing power across verticals. The commodity nature of Cintas’ services and the number of alternatives limit its ability to take price. Uniform rental, restroom supplies, and first aid services are necessary, but there is nothing proprietary. The company has been disciplined with pricing, historically contributing just 0–2% to annual growth. That restraint likely reflects management’s awareness that customers have options and can take these services in-house if pushed too hard.

Valuation - Reality Check

Cintas is clearly a quality company, one that has executed well, driving returns on invested capital to 28%. The question I am asking is not whether the business works, but whether the current price already reflects that strength.

To evaluate the valuation, I modeled Cintas using assumptions that reflect the continuation of strong execution.

  • Revenue: I assume mid- to high-single-digit revenue growth for the next 10 years, which translates to revenue doubling by 2035. This reflects continued success converting “No-Programmers,” sustained cross-selling, particularly in the First Aid & Safety segment, and ongoing tuck-in acquisitions. This acknowledges, however, that without pricing power, sustained acceleration beyond historic norms around 7% seems unlikely.

  • Margins: I assume operating margins gradually expand from roughly 23% today toward 27% over time. This gives credit to route density benefits, but also acknowledges that margin expansion becomes harder as the business matures.

  • Capital Intensity: I assume CapEx remains in the 3%–4% of revenue range and working capital remains stable relative to growth.

Under these assumptions, free cash flow compounds at approximately 10% annually. Including roughly 1% annual support from share repurchases, this translates to similar long-term EPS growth.

Fair Value Estimate

Using an 8% discount rate and an aggressive 4% terminal growth rate, the model produces an intrinsic value of approximately $171 per share. This implies a roughly 14% discount to today’s price.

The Disconnect

At current levels, the market appears to be pricing in a much more optimistic scenario.

If we reverse the DCF, current levels imply that the market is assuming sustained mid-double-digit FCF growth for a decade, or applying a higher terminal rate than modeled here. That requires revenue to accelerate beyond historic norms, margins to expand well into the high 20s, and no economic disruptions.

At 40x forward earnings, returns are driven almost entirely by earnings growth. There is no room for multiple expansion, and any compression toward historical averages would meaningfully impact shareholder returns.

Unifirst

There is one variable not reflected in the base case: the successful acquisition of UniFirst, Cintas’ largest competitor. In late 2025, Cintas resubmitted their proposal to buy UniFirst for $5.2 billion. If integrated efficiently, Cintas could recognize meaningful synergies by closing the margin gap between UniFirst’s 7–8% margins and Cintas’s 23%. However, this acquisition is not guaranteed to close, and large integrations carry risk. Therefore, I view this scenario as optionality rather than justification for the current valuation.

Conclusion

Cintas is a high-quality compounder with a durable competitive position and disciplined management team. The business continues to execute well.

However, given the expectations priced into the stock, the risk/reward is just not there. I view Cintas as a business worth owning, but at the right price. For me, that price lies closer to $170, where expected returns better reflect both the quality of the business and a realistic growth profile.

data.title: Bank Of America: It Doesn't Have To Be An Or/Or Story
data.description:
Introduction

Despite being non-cumulative in nature, I still like the preferred shares issued by U.S. banks. As they represent senior equity on the balance sheet, and considering most banks are known or would like to be known for reliable dividends on the common shares, I generally like the risk/reward ratio offered by the more senior equity.

Bank of America (BAC) doesn’t need a long introduction. I still like the bank’s somewhat busted preferred stock (BAC.PR.L), which I discussed in this article, but I think it also makes sense to keep an eye on the other preferred shares that are currently outstanding.

A strong net interest income

But to ensure the preferred dividend payments are safe, I am keeping an eye on the quarterly results of Bank of America. Although I’m not too worried about the non-cumulative nature of the preferred stock, I always want to make sure an issuer is in good financial health.

And Bank of America didn’t disappoint. As the income statement below shows, the bank reported a net interest income of $15.8 billion in the final quarter of 2025, which represents a 3.5% increase on a QoQ basis, and an increase of almost 10% compared to the final quarter of 2024. A remarkable result, indeed.

And that net interest income improvement was quite welcome, as the non-interest income came in at the lowest level of all four quarters in 2025. This was mainly due to a substantial decrease in market-making activities, and this certainly weighed on the bottom-line result. As the non-interest expenses overview below shows, the lower market making income did not result in a lower non-interest expense.

This means the pre-tax income before taking loan loss provisions into account was approximately $10.9 billion. And although the bank didn’t perform very well in its market-making division, I am very pleased with some other elements in its income statement. As you can see, the $1.3 billion loan loss provision remained pretty stable on a QoQ basis, and is almost 15% lower than the provisioning rate in the first two quarters of the year.

A certain author has been ringing the alarm bell for U.S. banks for quite a while now, but BAC has been able to keep its loan loss provisions pretty limited. And even if the situation were to get tougher, the bank’s $10.9 billion pre-provision pre-tax income can absorb much higher provisions before posting a loss.

In the final quarter of 2025, the net profit came in at $7.65 billion, of which $328 million was needed to cover the preferred dividends. This means the bank needed less than 5% of its net profit to cover these dividends, although there also is a timing issue, and preferred dividend payments are higher in Q1 and Q3, and lower in Q2 and Q4, as some issues only pay their dividends on a semi-annual basis.

But even if you look at the full-year results (which, of course, take the impact of the timing of the payments into consideration), the bank reported a $30.5 billion net income before preferred dividends, while it only required $1.5 billion to effectively ensure the preferred dividends are covered. So despite the timing of the effective payments, the payout ratio decreased to 4.77% in FY 2025, compared to 6.04% in 2024.

Revisiting fixed income securities

When looking at preferred shares, it’s always an interesting thinking exercise to pick the "right" preferred stock. Preferred shares with a higher coupon risk getting called but sometimes offer a good return if they indeed do get called. But I’m also interested in preferred stock with a low dividend coupon. Not only is the call risk negligible, but it also offers an opportunity to generate capital gains: lower interest rates on the financial markets should push the prices of bonds and fixed-rate preferred shares higher (providing the perceived risk of the issuing company remains unchanged).

Bank of America’s preferred shares Series PP ( BAC.PR.P) offer a fixed preferred dividend of 4.125% based on the principal value of $25 per share. The preferred dividend is paid on a quarterly basis, with just under $0.258 per share payable. As these securities were issued when the interest rates on the financial markets were much lower, the Series PP preferred stock is now trading at a discount of in excess of 30% to the $25 principal value.

Using the current share price of around $17.20, the Series PP preferred stock offers a dividend yield of just under 6%. This represents a markup of almost 240 bps to the 5-year U.S. Treasury yield .

Investment thesis

While Bank of America saw its EPS increase by approximately 20% to $3.86 in FY 2025, the stock isn’t very cheap, as it is trading at approximately 14 times earnings. However, looking at the consensus estimates for the net income for the next few years, we see the consensus estimates calling for an EPS of $4.34 this year, increasing to $4.96 next year. This means the current share price represents an earnings multiple of 10.5 based on next year’s consensus estimates. That makes the stock a light "Buy" at the current share price, but weakness in its share price could be an opportunity.

Meanwhile, the bank’s preferred shares continue to offer an attractive risk/reward opportunity for income-focused investors.

data.title: Walmart Earnings, FOMC Meeting Minutes In Focus Next Week
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street’s major market averages observed limited movements on Friday as traders digested the latest round of key retail inflation data.
