data.title: Given The Nvidia Tax, Broadcom And Marvell Offer A Powerful 1-2 AI Punch
data.description:
A couple of months back, I reviewed Nvidia and came away with a hold rating despite being impressed on various accounts. The challenge, in my opinion, is that Nvidia’s (NASDAQ:NVDA) net income margins are so high and its stranglehold on AI compute is so strong that it will almost inevitably create pushback from AI developers, like Google and OpenAI. We’re seeing this play out with various efforts to launch competing chips, including in-house chips for Google and the like. For now, Nvidia is likely to maintain its moat, but the competition is making headway.

Ultimately, the "Nvidia tax" will continue to fuel efforts to diversify AI chips, which in turn should provide Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) and Marvell (NASDAQ:MRVL) with strong, sustained tailwinds. If nothing else, more chip options will keep Nvidia honest and will likely trim net income margins over time, although ultimately, I do believe the chip industry will diversify and Broadcom and Marvell should be among the biggest winners.

There is a lot to like with both Broadcom and Marvell and I could justify investing in both. That said, there are a few serious concerns with Broadcom's valuation and fundamentals. With Marvell, I see no major hangups. Also, Broadcom is a far larger and more diversified company, making it less of a pure play and likely weakening the impact of the Nvidia tax. Marvell is more of an AI chip and data center infrastructure pure play and I reckon it has higher upside, but also a greater risk profile and lower floor. Given the speed and potential for the Artificial Intelligence industry, I’m comfortable taking on higher risks in exchange for higher upside. As a smaller company, if Marvell can tap into the opportunities provided by the Nvidia tax, it could move the needle substantially.

Overall, I believe Marvell offers the better investment opportunity. I don't think Broadcom is a bad investment, per se, and I can see why investors may want to invest in both. Doing so would offer an opportunity to hedge against risks. These companies clash in certain battlegrounds, such as designing custom chips. If one does begin to pull ahead at the expense of the other, share price gains for the leading company could offset share price declines for the laggard. Still, in terms of potential performance and fundamentals, Marvell takes the lead in my book.

The New Tea Party? Dumping the Nvidia Tax

It’s pretty much impossible to not be impressed by Nvidia’s fundamentals. Nvidia’s net income margin currently tops 50%, while its gross profit margin comes in at 70%. Since the gross profit margin doesn’t include marketing costs and the like, the relatively narrow spread between the net income margin and gross income margin suggests that Nvidia doesn’t have to put as many resources into non-COGS expenses as is typical. The ongoing chip shortages mean that Nvidia can’t produce enough to fully meet demand. Going back to economics 101, when demand outstrips supply, prices tend to go up.

Most sources point to Nvidia controlling 90% or more of the AI training market. One source claims that Nvidia’s AI training market share has declined from 72% in 2023 to 58% in 2025, with AMD controlling 22% and custom-chips (e.g. TPUs) accounting for 18% (2025). Market share data can be murky in many industries, and companies working on AI in particular often remain intentionally vague. I’d be careful about assigning too much credence to any specific numbers, but no matter what way you cut it, Nvidia is the dominant AI chip provider.

Right now, one of the best Nvidia challengers is Alphabet, which uses AI-specific Tensor Processing Units (TPUs) both for training and inference. Alphabet believes that its TPUs could take 10% of Nvidia’s sales. Reportedly, OpenAI at one point threatened to switch to Alphabet TPUs, forcing Nvidia to cut prices by 30%. (There are reports of this, but I wouldn’t treat it as confirmed.) Alphabet’s chips were designed in partnership with Broadcom. UBS reports that Alphabet TPUs will likely account for $30 billion in revenue for Broadcom this year. Further, shipments of TPUs will grow from 3.7 million this year to 5 million in 2027. As of 2025, however, only about 31% of Broadcom's revenues come from AI. Thus, even as AI revenues grow, the overall impact on Broadcom will be weakened to some extent. Broadcom derives over 40% of its revenues from software, including cybersecurity and automation software.

Broadcom's partnership with Alphabet is the most attractive card in its hand. However, it's been rumored in the past that Alphabet would switch to Marvell as its primary supplier and partner. This has yet to come to pass, but I suspect that Alphabet used the threat as leverage (similar to how OpenAI used Alphabet as leverage against Nvidia). Alphabet has brought on a new partner in MediaTek to handle some aspects of the TPU. This move was made to diversify Alphabet's supply chain and lower costs. I wouldn't be surprised either if Marvell does eventually get looped in. Thus, while the partnership with Alphabet is enticing, it also leaves me wary.

Marvell, meanwhile, is more heavily concentrated in AI and data centers, accounting for roughly three-quarters of the company's revenues. As such, the massive AI CapEx boom and efforts to reduce the Nvidia tax could dramatically move the needle. Marvell has partnered with Amazon to help design and produce its custom Inferentia and Trainium chips. With Amazon looking to rapidly expand its AI footprint with $200 billion in CapEx spending this year, Marvell could be in line for generous paydays. Amazon is mostly using these chips for internal applications as far as I can tell. Anthropic does use Amazon’s chips, but they also use Alphabet TPUs and Nvidia chips.

As with Alphabet, Amazon is also looking to diversify its supply chain and bring in more partners. This should lower costs and provide leverage for Amazon. Rumors circulated in December 2025 that Marvell was losing Amazon's Trainium 3 and 4 designs. It was also claimed that Microsoft was moving to Broadcom. Microsoft is Marvell's second biggest customer, and it may also be looking to diversify its supply chain, but that doesn't mean Marvell will lose all of its business. Marvell's CEO refuted both accounts, and analysts at JP Morgan have echoed the claims. That said, AIchip Technologies is being brought in to handle at least some of the work with Amazon's AI chips.

Custom chips, including Alphabet's and Amazon’s chips, tend to be more energy efficient because they are designed specifically for AI. Nvidia’s AI chips evolved from more general GPUs popular in gaming and the like. Energy costs could influence the equation and encourage custom-chip adoption. While I am focusing on chips in this article, both Broadcom and Marvell provide other products and services. Marvell is a leading Digital Signal Processor provider, for example, enabling rapid data transfer within AI centers.

Broadcom and Marvell Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

As far as fundamentals are concerned, I believe both Broadcom and Marvell are generally pretty attractive, but MRVL comes away with some edges in key areas. I’ll also compare both stocks to Nvidia. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools give both companies F grades when it comes to valuation and I can see why the data points that way. However, we live in an era of high valuations. That said, Broadcom’s ~72 PE GAAP TTM ratio, shown above, is especially high. Marvell looks like a downright bargain compared to both Nvidia and Broadcom. Both Broadcom and Nvidia have had far higher PE ratios before, as illustrated below.

Another area of concern valuation wise is the Price/Sales ratio. Broadcom and Nvidia both have PS ratios of about 25, which is exceptionally high. Marvell is far more reasonable at 9, which is a touch high but pretty normal for a tech company. Broadcom would be an easy buy for me with a PS ratio of around 10 and a PE ratio of around 30. Unfortunately, however, that's not the case in the real world, and while I believe Broadcom has upside, it's not as extensive as Marvell's.

All three companies are enjoying strong revenue growth, but Marvell is growing much more quickly. Broadcom has the lowest growth, under 30%. Granted, Broadcom is a much larger company than Marvell. The lower baseline with Marvell can distort growth in some ways, and it’s often harder for larger companies to secure rapid growth in terms of percentages. Nvidia is an exception to this, of course, but no one should be surprised by that.

Nvidia also enjoys the highest net income margin. Indeed, its 53% net income margin is extremely high for a company of such size. Marvell and Broadcom aren’t slouches, and they’re highly profitable even by the high standards of tech stocks, but they lag far behind Nvidia. Yet that’s also why I am wary of the Nvidia tax and why I believe Marvell and Broadcom will enjoy tailwinds. Especially with Broadcom and given Alphabet's moves to bring in MediaTek, I would recommend keeping a close eye on profitability. Broadcom may need to shrink its margins with Alphabet's business in order to remain in its good graces. Right now, the high profit margins should be priced into Broadcom's share prices, so if margins do shrink, it could have an outsized impact.

Broadcom does have a bit more debt than I wanted to see, especially compared to the other two. The debt doesn’t strike me as a serious risk, but it needs to be taken into account with the valuation. All in all, these three companies strike me as healthy as far as the balance sheet goes.

The quant tools give all three companies a hold rating, with valuation getting poor marks across the board. My fellow Seeking Alpha analysts are more optimistic with buy ratings across the board. While these stocks aren’t cheap and the valuations are high, so long as the AI race continues, I believe Broadcom and Marvell will rise. Still, given Marvell's more favorable valuation metrics and its position as an AI pure play, if I had to pick one stock or the other, I'd go with MRVL. I’m a bit wary of Nvidia owing to the Nvidia tax and last rated it as a hold a few months ago.

Why I Could Be Wrong

The strongest part of Nvidia’s moat, in my opinion, isn’t actually its hardware but instead its CUDA software. From what I understand, this software is especially crucial in the training phase and is the most widely used platform. Switching to a new platform would be immensely difficult and would consume a lot of resources. Many are concerned that the AI race is evolving into a bubble. If this turns out to be true, Marvell and Broadcom would likely rank among the hardest hit. Broadcom is more diversified in some ways, and so that might mitigate some of the damage. However, Broadcom is still largely focused on AI data centers and would be hit hard.

Both Marvell and Broadcom are dependent on a small number of customers. As a result, said customers have a lot of leverage, and clearly, some are looking to rein in costs. If either company loses a major contract, it could tank their share prices. Right now, the AI race is so white hot and resources across the industry are stretched so thin that I believe this risk is muted. However, margins may come under pressure.

Further, I’d be careful about writing China off. As of now, it’s believed that China is well behind the United States in terms of chip design and the like. However, China does have a lot of resources, and when the Chinese government prioritizes something, they often find success. While I doubt China will catch up any time soon, I wouldn’t be shocked if they mount a stronger-than-expected challenge.

Key Takeaway: MRVL Takes The Edge

So long as the AI race continues at the current pace, I believe Broadcom and Marvell will tick upward. Still, Broadcom's PE and PS ratios are substantially higher than I'd want to see, and I believe its margins will come under pressure more quickly owing to Alphabet bringing MediaTek on board. The AI race is drawing in so much cash that I could justify buying Broadcom stocks, but overall, I believe Marvell has the edge. As an AI pure play, I also expect the AI tailwinds to have a larger impact on Marvell.

As for Nvidia, I suspect custom chips will nibble away at its market share going forward. If the CUDA moat is ever breached, Nvidia could come under immense pressure (that's a big if, however).
